title: Reconstructing Rage: Transformative Reentry in the Era of Mass Incarceration By Townsand Price-Spratlen and William Goldsby Peter Lang. 2012. 294 pages. $38.95 paper
abstract: Reconstructing Rage: Transformative Reentry in the Era of Mass Incarceration presents a case study and model for post-prison reentry programs in the age of mass incarceration. Reconstruction, the unit of analysis, can be best described as an Afrocentric, grassroots reentry organization dedicated to tempering and transforming the rage experienced and projected by Black men who have been imprisoned for violent offenses. The authors suggest that rage is spawned from community, family, and individual life disruption, exclusion, and oppression, as well as a lack of emotional intelligence to temper such sentiments. These factors, especially when experienced concurrently, are believed to lead to explosiveness that can result in violent crime. The authors suggest that while rage is indeed venomous, posing a hazard to individuals and communities, it can also be the antidote and solution to community problems when it is properly transformed into a capacity building resource. As such, Reconstruction seeks to provide a “homeplace” where Black people can safely affirm one another and heal their wounds.
